    Sprint 2 – Agents & Agentic Systems

    📄

    Patterns for building Agentic Systems

    Lesson 3

    In general, Agentic Systems are AI systems where a large language model (LLM) can autonomously reason and act. However, more recently, there was an attempt to distinguish Agentic Systems to specifically represent more deterministic Agentic Workflows while Agentic AI would represent multi-agent autonomous systems. In this lesson, we focus exclusively on single-agent design (multiple agents interacting will be covered later) and emphasize robust yet simple workflows for effective agents. Consistent with beliefs of the majority of the industry, the most successful agent implementations often favor simple, composable patterns over overly complex frameworks. I also find this to be true, while multi-agent, fully autonomous systems might be the future, we are not yet ready to build such systems in a reliable way. Goal of this article is to outline these simple patterns and the architectural principles behind them.


    At a high level, Agentic Workflows are LLM-driven processes constrained by predefined code paths, whereas agents are systems where the LLM dynamically decides what actions to take and how to use tools, steering its own process. In practice, both workflows and agents involve an LLM augmented with tools or external data sources - but agents grant the LLM greater autonomy in decision-making.



    Principles for Designing Effective Agentic Systems


    Before constructing an agent, it’s crucial to consider when an agentic approach is appropriate and how to keep its design effective. Not every problem needs an autonomous agent - often a simpler solution suffices. As Anthropic’s guide advises, start with the simplest possible approach and only introduce complexity if needed. Agentic systems can trade off greater task success for higher latency and cost, so the benefits should justify the overhead. In fact, for many applications a single LLM call augmented with retrieval (and perhaps a few in-context examples) is usually enough. Always ask yourself: Can a straightforward retrieval-augmented prompt solve this? If yes, you might not need a complex agent loop.


    When the task requirements do call for more sophistication (e.g. dynamic decision-making, tool use, or multi-step reasoning that can’t be hardcoded), you have a choice between implementing a fixed workflow or giving more autonomy to an agent. Workflows offer predictability and consistency by following predefined logic, whereas agents provide flexibility and adaptability by letting the model choose its path. In practice, you might start with a structured workflow for well-understood tasks, and only move to an unbound agent approach for open-ended tasks where the sequence of steps can’t be predetermined.


    Another principle is simplicity of design. Complex agent frameworks exist, but adding layers of abstraction too early can backfire. Anthropic notes that while frameworks like LangGraph make it easy to prototype, they can hide the underlying prompt/response details and lead developers to lose clarity on what the model is actually doing (this is also one of the reasons why we are using LangGraph for piping only and still utilise Instructor for structured outputs and retries. We also construct the messages ourselves). A common mistake is assuming the framework “magic” will solve problems, when in reality it’s just wrapping patterns you could implement yourself. In short, do try to always understand your tools: if you use an agent framework, ensure you know how it works under the hood. Up until now I tried to stress architecture transparency - you should be able to explain how your agent’s prompt and code lead it to act.


    In summary, the design philosophy is: keep it as simple as possible, add complexity only with evidence it improves outcomes, and maintain transparency and control. Specifically, Anthropic’s best practices distill to three core principles:


    • Maintain simplicity in the agent’s design. Prefer straightforward workflows over convoluted logic. Each added component or loop should have a clear purpose.

    • Prioritize transparency in the agent’s reasoning. The agent should externalize its plans or thoughts (e.g. via a scratchpad or verbose logging), so that both developers and the model itself can track what’s happening. This helps with debugging and trust.

    • Carefully craft the agent-computer interface (ACI). In other words, design your tools and APIs that the agent will use with as much care as you design the prompts. A well-documented, easy-to-use toolset makes the agent’s job easier and safer. We’ll discuss tool interfaces more later (in the upcoming MCP lesson), but the takeaway is to make tool usage obvious to the model and thoroughly test how the agent uses each tool.


    Adhering to these principles will produce agents that are not only powerful but also reliable, maintainable, and controllable. With these guidelines in mind, let’s move on to the concrete architecture patterns for single-agent systems, from simple to more complex.



    Common Single-Agent Workflow Patterns


    Now we move from components to patterns - repeatable architectures for using an LLM (with tools/memory) to accomplish tasks. These patterns range from straightforward sequences to more complex ones. They were hinted at in the famous Anthropic article as well as industry practice, and they serve as building blocks for agentic systems. We’ll cover five major workflow patterns below, all within the scope of a single-agent (one principal reasoning LLM guiding the process):



    Prompt Chaining


    Prompt chaining is one of the simplest patterns: it breaks a complex task into a linear sequence of subtasks, each handled by an LLM (or a tool) in turn. For example, if the overall task is to produce a well-researched report, you might chain: (Step 1) generate an outline, (Step 2) verify the outline meets requirements, (Step 3) fill in one section of the report, (Step 4) fill in the next section, etc., and finally (Step N) compile the full report. Each step’s prompt can include the output of the previous step, thereby passing context along the chain.


    The advantage of prompt chaining is that each LLM call can focus on a simpler sub-problem, potentially increasing accuracy. Instead of asking one model invocation to do everything, you make the job easier by splitting the work into stages. This trades off additional latency (multiple calls) for higher reliability or quality. It’s useful when tasks naturally break into clear phases or when intermediate validation is needed. In the figure above (inspired by Anthropic’s diagram), you see a “gate” after an intermediate step. This gate represents a programmatic check or criteria to ensure the subtask’s output is acceptable before proceeding. For instance, after generating an outline, you might have a function that checks “Does the outline cover all required topics?” - if not, the chain could revise the outline or abort.




    When to use prompt chaining: use it if you can identify a stable sequence of subtasks that every input should go through. The pattern works best in workflows like content generation with review steps, multi-stage data processing, or any scenario where you want to systematically refine an answer. Some examples include:


    • Writing something in stages: e.g. first draft a response, then have another step summarize it, then another step translate it.

    • Complex Q&A: first use one prompt to extract key entities from the question (or a document), next use those to query a knowledge base, then another to formulate the final answer.

    • Validation workflows: e.g. an LLM generates code, then a second LLM (or tool) tests or checks that code, then maybe a third LLM fixes any errors.


    Prompt chaining is highly modular - each step can be developed and tested in isolation. Using frameworks like LangGraph, you could implement each subtask as a node and explicitly chain them in a graph (remember that LangGraph nodes do not need to have LLM logic in them). This pattern is still mostly a workflow rather than a free-form agent, because the path is predetermined. However, it sets the stage for more adaptive patterns by showing how outputs and inputs can flow through a pipeline.



    Routing


    Not all inputs should be handled the same way. Routing is a pattern where the agent first classifies or decides what kind of request it is dealing with, and then routes the request to a specialised process flow or prompt for that category. In other words, there is an initial router step (which could be an LLM or simple logic) that chooses among multiple possible subsequent chains or tools.


    For example, you could imagine an AI assistant that handles both questions about the inventory and intent of buying some of the items. A routing step (intent router) could analyze the user’s query - if it’s an inquiry type of question, route it to a workflow that uses an inventory knowledge base, whereas if it’s a buying intent question, route to a heavier agentic system capable of managing the user’s shopping cart. Anthropic describes this as enabling separation of concerns - each type of query is handled by a dedicated prompt or toolset, so you can optimize each without one logic confusing the other. 




    When to use routing: this pattern is ideal for heterogeneous task sets where a one-size-fits-all approach would be suboptimal. If you find yourself writing an overly complex prompt trying to cover diverse scenarios, consider splitting the logic. Routing can be done by an LLM (e.g. a small classification prompt: “Is this input Type A, B, or C?”) or by external rules (e.g. regex or model confidence thresholds). Some examples:


    • Customer support bots that route inventory questions vs. reviews questions vs. complaints to different answer workflows.

    • A system that chooses different LLM models based on difficulty: e.g. simple questions go to a cheaper, faster model, while hard ones go to a more powerful model. I’ve witnessed multiple systems benefiting from this, especially when the more powerful model can also be a model that has been fine tuned for a specific set of hard questions.

    • Route the initial question through a router that decides if it can answer the question with the first pass if it is a general question or needs any additional agency capabilities, then routing to a predefined workflow.


    In terms of architecture, a router can be seen as an initial node that branches the graph. In tools like LangGraph this would mean a conditional edge which implements this cleanly. The takeaway is that routing keeps your agent modular - each branch can be developed and tuned somewhat independently. It also adds predictability - you know that certain inputs will only trigger certain parts of the system. However, routing relies on the initial classification being correct - if the router fails, the query might go down the wrong path. Therefore, invest in a reliable routing criterion (which might include confidence checks or a fallback to a human or a default flow if uncertain).



    Parallelization


    Sometimes a task can be naturally split into parts that can be solved independently (and faster in parallel), or you may want multiple attempts at the same task to improve confidence. Parallelization is the pattern of running multiple LLM calls concurrently and then aggregating their results. Anthropic highlights two common variants of this pattern:


    • Sectioning: Divide the task into independent chunks and process them in parallel. For instance, if summarizing a long document, you might break the text into sections and have multiple LLM instances summarize different sections at the same time. Another example is a complex form that needs to be filled - separate agents fill different parts simultaneously and then a final step combines the parts. Or even a report creation could be handled in parallel, potentially for each section in parallel.

    • Voting (or n-best): Have the agent (or multiple agents) attempt the same task multiple times, or with slight prompt variations, to generate a set of candidate answers. Then use a procedure to pick the best result or an ensemble decision. For example, you could prompt an LLM three times for a critical answer and take a majority vote or use another model to judge the best answer.


    Parallelization trades off computation for speed or confidence. If you have the resources, running things in parallel can greatly reduce latency for multi-step tasks (since steps that don’t depend on each other can happen simultaneously). It can also improve results by combining outputs. However, combining results means you need a reliable aggregation mechanism (e.g. concatenating section summaries is straightforward, but merging answers from different attempts might need a scoring function or a final “judge” LLM).




    When to use parallelization: use it when sub-parts of a problem truly don’t depend on each other (or can be made independent), or when you want multiple perspectives. Some concrete scenarios:


    • Implementing AI guardrails: One model handles the user’s query while simultaneously another model (or process) checks the query or the draft answer for safety/policy compliance. This parallel approach often works better than forcing one LLM to do both tasks at once. Once both are complete, you can aggregate by checking if the question is compliant and returning the answer to the processed query in such a case only.

    • LLM evaluation: For instance, to evaluate an agent’s output, you could have several LLMs score it on different criteria in parallel (accuracy, clarity, politeness, etc.) and then aggregate those scores.

    • Diverse brainstorming: Ask an LLM to generate multiple different solutions in parallel, then either pick the best or combine them. This is like an automated divergent thinking process.

    • Redundancy for reliability: For critical computations, run two instances of the same task and compare results - if they differ, you know there’s uncertainty.


    Architecturally, parallelization can be implemented by launching multiple asynchronous tools or LLM calls. In LangGraph terms, that might mean multiple nodes at the same stage of the graph that later join. The results can be stored in state and then a subsequent node can handle aggregation. Make sure that the overhead of combining results doesn’t nullify the speed gains. Also, ensure each parallel branch has the needed context (they might each need the original prompt or relevant subset of data).



    Orchestrator-Workers


    A more sophisticated single-agent pattern involves an orchestrator LLM that dynamically breaks a task into parts and assigns those parts to other LLM calls as “workers”. This is similar to a manager delegating subtasks to employees and then aggregating the results. The Orchestrator-Workers workflow has the central agent planning and distributing work in real-time, rather than following a fixed predetermined chain.


    How is this different from simple prompt chaining or parallelization? The key is that the subtasks are not fixed ahead of time, the orchestrator decides what needs to be done based on the input or intermediate results. For example, consider a coding agent tasked with implementing a complex feature. An orchestrator LLM might analyze the request and decide it needs to modify five different files. It can then spawn 5 separate LLM calls (workers), each tasked with modifying a specific file, possibly all running in parallel. Once they finish, the orchestrator can call another step to integrate or verify the changes. If new subtasks emerge (e.g. tests failing), the orchestrator can create new tasks. This is much more flexible than a fixed chain of “first do X, then Y”, because the number and nature of subtasks can vary per task.


    Another scenario is a research agent - given a question, an orchestrator could decide to launch multiple search queries via a search-tool LLM, each focusing on a different aspect of the question, then gather those results and have another step synthesize an answer.




    When to use orchestrator-workers: this pattern excels when you cannot predict the sub-problems in advance, or when the task naturally decomposes differently for each input. Anthropic notes it’s useful for tasks like coding, where the scope (# of files, functions affected) varies every time. It’s also useful in open-ended search or analysis tasks where the number of sources or pieces of information needed isn’t fixed.


    In terms of design, orchestrator-workers can be seen as a hierarchical agent - a “boss” agent and multiple “assistant” agents (though all are LLMs, possibly even the same model). However, since this is a single-agent system lesson, we conceptually still treat the orchestrator as the agent - it’s orchestrating multiple LLM calls as part of its process, not negotiating with independent peer agents. If using LangGraph, you might implement this by having a loop where an LLM (orchestrator) node decides on next actions, and can spawn subgraph executions for each subtask, then collects results. This can get complex, so start simple - perhaps implement a two-level system (one orchestrator call, a fixed number of worker calls, then a synthesis call). 


    One challenge is ensuring workers have precise instructions. The orchestrator needs to generate a clear spec for each worker (which could be a prompt that the worker LLM will see). This is essentially planning and requires careful prompt design so that the workers know their role and output format. The benefit is a very powerful and flexible agent architecture, but the cost is complexity in prompt orchestration and increased difficulty in tracing errors (you have multiple LLM calls that could go wrong). Proper logging of each worker’s output and the orchestrator’s decisions is essential for debugging.



    Evaluator-Optimizer Loop


    The final pattern we will discuss is an Evaluator-Optimizer loop. This involves at least two distinct LLM roles: one generates an initial answer or solution, and another evaluates that output and provides feedback or critique, which the first then uses to improve its next attempt (we have actually covered most of this in previous week’s materials). Essentially, the agent performs a task, self-judges or self-critiques it, and iterates. You can think of it as the agent splitting itself into a “writer” and an “editor”.


    This pattern is inspired by human processes like writing drafts and revising after feedback. For instance, the agent could attempt to answer a question, then another LLM call checks whether the answer meets certain criteria (accuracy, completeness, style guidelines, etc.). If not, the evaluator might suggest what’s wrong or what’s missing. The agent then uses that feedback in a next iteration to refine the answer. This loop can repeat multiple times or until a certain quality threshold is met. It’s somewhat similar to how humans use tools like Grammarly or peer review to refine a piece of text.




    When to use evaluator-optimizer: when the task has clear evaluation criteria or when iterative refinement adds value. Good fits are tasks where a first-pass solution often needs improvement and where you can formalize what “better” means. Anthropic suggests scenarios like:


    • Complex writing or translation: e.g., a literary translation agent could draft a translation, then another LLM judges if it preserved nuances, then the agent revises accordingly.

    • Research or fact-finding: an agent answers a question by searching sources; an evaluator agent checks if the answer is well-supported and if not, prompts further searches.

    • Coding tasks: one LLM writes code, another reviews it or runs tests to point out bugs, then the first fixes them (this is what coding agents do a lot).


    This pattern requires that the LLM is capable of critiquing output - which the latest models often can do. It is effectively a special case of chain-of-thought where the “thought” now includes a critical reflection step.


    From an architecture viewpoint, this can be implemented in a loop - call the “solver” LLM for an output, then call a separate “evaluator” LLM with both the input and the output to get feedback, then feed that feedback plus the previous output back into the solver (perhaps as part of the prompt) for a revision. Then repeat this. Ensure that you have a termination condition: for example, stop after a fixed number of iterations or once the evaluator says “looks good”. This avoids infinite loops where an evaluator might always find something to complain about.


    One thing to be cautious about: the evaluator’s feedback needs to be understandable and usable by the solver. If the feedback is too vague (“this answer isn’t good”), it won’t help, if it is too harsh or incorrect, it might degrade the solution. Designing the evaluator’s prompt to focus on constructive, specific feedback is key.



    Composing and Customizing Patterns


    The patterns described above are not mutually exclusive - they are building blocks that you can combine in various ways to fit your use case. For example, you might have a routing step, and within one branch you use a prompt chain, while another branch uses an evaluator loop. Or an autonomous agent might internally use a chain or an evaluator step as part of its dynamic workflow.


    The ability to mix patterns is where frameworks like LangGraph or general workflow engines really help, because you can represent complex flows that branch, loop, and rejoin. However, even if coding manually, you can still modularize - write separate functions for each sub-pattern and then call them appropriately. The modular architecture approach means each part of your agent’s workflow can be developed and tested independently before integration.


    While it’s tempting to combine all patterns and make a super-agent, it’s better to start with one pattern and measure performance. If it falls short on some criteria, then consider adding another pattern to address that deficiency. For instance, you start with a straightforward chain. If you find the outputs vary in quality, you might add a voting parallelization to pick the best output. Or if you find the chain’s final answer is sometimes flawed, you could append an evaluator loop to refine it. This way, you incrementally build up to a more complex system while ensuring each addition brings a measurable improvement.


    In summary, think of these patterns as building blocks. You can assemble different shapes of agent workflows from them.



    Memory and Scratchpad Usage


    We touched on memory earlier, but it’s worth diving deeper into how an agent can remember and how that affects its capabilities. Memory in agentic systems comes in a few flavors, and using them effectively makes your agent more powerful and consistent:


    • Short-Term Memory (Contextual): This is everything the agent can remember within the prompt context of ongoing interactions. It includes the conversation history with the user, the agent’s own chain-of-thought log (scratchpad), and recent tool outputs. Essentially, it’s the working memory that is carried from one cycle of the agent loop to the next. This memory is ephemeral - if the conversation or session ends, it’s gone unless saved externally - and is limited by the LLM’s context window (e.g., 128K token limit). A key skill in agent design is managing this short-term memory: deciding what to keep, what to summarize, and what to discard as interactions progress. If the agent has too little context, it will forget important details - too much, and there is a risk of hitting length limits or diluting the prompt focus.

    • Long-Term Memory (Persistent): This refers to information retained across sessions or too large to hold in a single prompt. Typically implemented with external storage (databases, vector stores, files), long-term memory allows the agent to learn from past experiences or store knowledge it might need later. For example, after solving a problem, an agent might store a summary of the solution so it can recall it if a similar problem arises. Or an agent might have a profile of the user’s preferences stored to personalize responses. Implementing long-term memory requires tools - e.g., a memory retrieval tool that the agent can query. It might also involve writing to memory (the agent decides to save something). In practice, a common pattern is: the agent has a vector database of notes, and at the start of a session or when needed, it does a similarity search to fetch any relevant notes from past interactions (this is like giving the agent experience).

    • Scratchpad (Intermediate Reasoning Log): We’ve mentioned this multiple times - it’s essentially part of short-term memory, but deserves special treatment. The scratchpad is where the agent writes down its chain-of-thought and actions. The scratchpad serves multiple purposes: (1) it helps the agent not repeat itself - the model can “see” that it already tried a certain strategy if it wrote it down, (2) it allows the agent to compute or derive intermediate results step by step (like working out parts of a math problem across multiple thoughts) and (3) it provides a trace for debugging - as developers, we can read the scratchpad and follow the agent’s reasoning, spotting where it might have gone wrong. When building your agent, think about how you will capture this internal state. If using a framework like LangGraph, in most cases it would automatically log each node’s input/output. If done manually, you’ll accumulate a string. Either way, ensure the scratchpad is included in the next LLM call’s prompt so the agent effectively has a memory of what it has done.

    • Retrieval Augmentation vs. Memory: There’s a bit of overlap in terminology - some might call using a vector store “long-term memory”. The difference is subtle: retrieval (RAG) is usually about pulling in external knowledge that the agent didn’t generate itself, whereas memory often refers to things the agent did generate or encounter, stored for later. Functionally, both are about extending the context window with relevant info. Whether you call your knowledge store “memory” or just an external resource, the agent will use a similar interface (some search or lookup tool). Usually retrieval is used for semantic memory (important facts or conclusions extracted and saved) rather than just literal past dialogues.

    • Memory Management: Since short-term memory is limited, agents might need strategies to manage it. This could include (we covered most in previous week materials):


      • Summarization: Summarize old parts of the conversation or scratchpad when they get too long. This compresses information, though at the risk of losing detail.

      • Chunking: Only keep the last N interactions in a literal form and drop older ones beyond that, possibly relying on long-term memory for older context if needed.

      • Relevance filtering: Only keep information that seems relevant to the current goal. If the agent changed the topic, maybe previous content is not needed anymore.

      • These strategies can be automated or manual. For example, you might insert a step: “Summarize what has been accomplished so far” and then use that summary in place of a long trail of thoughts.


    • Working Memory Limits and Mistakes: Sometimes an agent will forget details from earlier in the conversation or contradict itself - this is often due to context limit or the model’s inherent limitations. No matter how good the memory system, if something critical is not in the prompt at inference time, the model cannot recall it. So design your memory tooling such that key facts or decisions are carried forward. If a user gives an important instruction in message 1 and the agent only needs it again by message 20, ensure either it remains in the short-term context or was stored and retrieved. Testing the agent on long sessions can surface these issues.


    As an example, consider a customer support agent that handles a long troubleshooting dialogue. Short-term memory is the recent conversation about the issue, long-term memory might be the customer’s profile or past issues (fetched from a database). The agent might also keep a scratchpad of steps it has tried (so it doesn’t repeat the same fix attempt twice). If the conversation drags on, the agent may summarize prior exchanges (“So far, we checked X and Y, which didn’t solve the issue”) to both remind itself and inform the user, rather than recalling every utterance. In sum, memory is what makes an agent stateful and enables it to continuously try and solve a problem.



    Termination Criteria and Safeguards


    Every agent needs to be told when to stop. Without clear termination criteria, an autonomous agent might loop forever or stop too early. Additionally, safeguards are necessary to prevent undesirable behaviors as the agent operates. Here are few methods on how to decide when an agent’s job is done and what controls to put in place:


    • Goal Completion: Ideally, the agent stops when it has achieved the user’s goal or answered the question. For a single-turn query (e.g. “What is this store specialising in”), the termination is simply providing the final answer. For multi-step tasks, the agent might have a criteria of when the task is completed. Often we incorporate into the prompt something like: “If you believe you have answered the question or completed the task, you should present the final answer and stop”. In practice, the agent will produce a special action or output to indicate the final answer. If using OpenAI function calling as an example, the agent signals it by returning to the assistant role with a normal message instead of a function call.

    • Maximum Iterations/Turns: To avoid infinite loops, impose a hard limit on the number of steps the agent can take. This could be 5 tool uses, or 10 reasoning loops, depending on how complex tasks are expected to get. If the agent hits the limit, it should either conclude with the best it has or escalate (perhaps ask for help or at least not continue blindly). Hitting the iteration cap often indicates the agent is stuck. You might design the agent in such a way that if it reaches max turns without a solution, it outputs something like “I’m sorry, I’m unable to find a solution within the allowed steps.” This is much preferable to it rambling or breaking. The exact number for the cap may need to be tuned.

    • Time or Cost Limits: Similarly, you might set a wall-clock timer or token usage limit. If an agent hasn’t finished by X seconds or used Y tokens, it stops. This is more relevant in long-running agents. Monitoring token usage is important if cost is a concern - an agent that keeps calling an expensive model in a loop could blow up the cost.

    • Quality-based Termination (Convergence): In evaluator-optimizer loops, you need a criterion for when to stop iterating. It could be a fixed number of iterations or it could be when the evaluator says “this is good enough”. For example, if the evaluator rates the answer 9/10 or explicitly says “All criteria satisfied”, the agent can stop refining. Similarly, in an orchestrator-worker scenario, maybe the orchestrator decides no more subtasks are needed. That decision is often encoded in the agent’s logic (perhaps the agent examines if all subtasks results meet some condition).

    • User Control: For interactive agents (like a chatbot that is also an agent), the user might stop the process. If the user asks for a follow-up or says “stop”, the agent should comply. Designing for this means the agent’s loop should always check if there is new user input or an abort signal at the top of each iteration.

    • Safeguards and Guardrails: Guardrails (we will go deeper into guardrails in week 7) ensure the agent doesn’t violate certain rules while trying to achieve its goal. This includes:


      • Content Safety: The agent should not produce disallowed content or take disallowed actions. If a user query steers the agent towards something harmful (or the agent’s own chain-of-thought starts going into a dangerous territory), the system should step in. Often a safety filter is run on either the inputs or the outputs (or both). For example, after the agent composes a final answer, you might check it against a list of prohibited content categories and refuse if it’s bad. Or you might have a parallel process (like mentioned in parallelization pattern) that monitors the conversation for policy violations.

      • Tool Use Restrictions: Certain tools should have restricted use. For example, an agent might have a file system tool but you only want it to access a sandbox directory. The system integrating the tool should enforce that (the agent might ask to open /etc/passwd but the tool should deny it if not allowed). Ideally, the agent is made aware of boundaries in the prompt (“You have access only to these files…”), but a secure implementation doesn’t rely solely on the agent following instructions - it also has checks.

      • Human Oversight Hooks: You could incorporate points where the agent pauses and asks for confirmation. This is more relevant in multi-turn or complex tasks. For example, an agent might generate a plan and then present it to a user: “Here is what I plan to do: Step 1, Step 2... Shall I proceed?” This is a design choice - it can greatly increase trust if a human can confirm a plan before execution. It does, however, break full autonomy and slows things down, so it depends on the use case.

      • Logging and Monitoring: From a design perspective, ensure you log the agent’s actions and thoughts. If it does something odd or goes in circles, these logs are how you’ll debug and improve the system. In production, monitoring these logs can sometimes catch a rogue agent before it causes a problem (for instance, if you see an agent stuck in a loop of “search again” 20 times, you might want to kill that process and investigate).


    Ideally, you would test agents in sandboxed environments with guardrails before deploying them broadly. A sandbox could be a controlled environment where even if the agent acts incorrectly, it will not have serious consequences (e.g. a fake account in a stage environment of a ticketing system). Only after it behaves correctly in sandbox tests (and with safety checks in place) would you deploy it more widely.


    Finally, consider fallback strategies. If the agent fails to complete the task (due to hitting limits or encountering errors it can’t resolve), what will you do then? In a user-facing scenario, maybe it should return with a partial answer or an apology and escalate to a human or a simpler system. In an automated backend scenario, maybe it triggers an alert. The key is to handle failure modes gracefully. A robust agent is not the one that never fails (all agents will have some failure cases), but one that fails safely and obviously (not silently producing a wrong result or hanging indefinitely). By clearly defining termination conditions and implementing safeguards, you ensure your single-agent system remains under control.
